_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the technical fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.cape: The Shiller CAPE (Cyclically Adjusted Price-to-Earnings ratio) averages inflation-adjusted earnings over the past 10 years to smooth business cycle effects. As of July 2026, the CAPE stands at 41.77, far above its 140-year average of 17.3 and approaching the all-time high recorded at the December 1999 dot-com peak of 44.19. Elevated CAPE levels indicate that stocks are expensive relative to long-term earnings, historically forecasting low or negative forward returns; for instance, readings above approximately 39 have correlated with average S&P 500 returns of -4% over one year and -20% over two years. However, this indicator does not specify when valuations will revert nor account for structural changes such as persistently low interest rates that may support higher multiples.
notes.buffett: The Buffett Indicator is the ratio of total US equity market capitalization to gross domestic product (GDP), a broad gauge of market valuation relative to economic output. In June 2026, it registered 231.7%, well above the dot-com peak of 146% and the 2007 peak of 110%. Warren Buffett has famously warned that readings above 200% represent a hazardous overvaluation zone, implying the market is 'playing with fire.' This metric matters because market cap far outpacing GDP suggests speculative excess and reduced margin of safety, often preceding market corrections. Nonetheless, shifts in globalization, corporate profit margins, or the role of intangible assets can complicate the direct GDP comparison and timing of corrections.
notes.concentration: Market concentration measures how much of the S&P 500's total market capitalization is held by its largest constituents, reflecting sectoral and name-specific risk. In 2026, the top five stocks account for approximately 29.8% of the index, the top ten for 41.0%, up sharply from 17.5% in 2015, while the technology sector alone comprises 48%. High concentration can amplify systemic risk since declines in a handful of companies or one sector can disproportionately impact overall index performance, reducing diversification benefits. However, concentration may also reflect innovation leadership and network effects, which can sustain high valuations over time, complicating timing forecasts based solely on this metric.
notes.yieldcurve: The yield curve spread between the 10-year Treasury note and 3-month Treasury bill is a common indicator of economic sentiment and recession risk, with a steeper positive spread generally signaling healthier growth expectations. As of July 2026, this spread is 0.69 percentage points, alongside a NY Federal Reserve recession probability model estimating a 31% likelihood of recession. A positive but modest spread suggests the bond market anticipates moderate growth with some risks, often correlating with elevated uncertainty that can pressure equity valuations. Yet yield curve signals can be influenced by central bank policy and external shocks, meaning they may not precisely time downturns but provide probabilistic risk context.
notes.drypowder: Dry powder represents the cash reserves held by investment entities ready for deployment into opportunities, in this case Berkshire Hathaway’s cash position stands at a record $397.4 billion as of Q1 2026. It signals potential market liquidity injections or firepower for acquisitions when valuations decline, often counterbalancing selling pressures. However, 79% of institutional investors expect cash positions to decrease, suggesting less liquidity available for market support in the near term. While ample dry powder can mitigate shocks when the music stops, timing is uncertain as capital deployment depends on market sentiment and confidence conditions.
notes.finale: Valuation metrics like CAPE and the Buffett Indicator set the magnitude and potential severity of a market downturn, indicating a richly valued, fragile environment. However, the precise timing of a transition from bull to bear markets hinges on liquidity shocks—moments when market participants rush for exits and the 'music stops.' A critical window for such a trigger exists between December 2026 and May 2027, coinciding with IPO lock-up expirations for major companies like SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic, which may introduce significant share supply and test investor demand. Thus, while current valuations suggest vulnerability, the timing and catalyst of a downturn depend heavily on market liquidity dynamics and investor responses to these structural dislocations.
